title: Environmental effects on phenotype
description: Test your knowledge of environmental effects on phenotype!

assessment_items.question_content:
Snowshoe hares (*Lepus americanus*) display a seasonal change in coat color from brown to white (Figure 1). This transition occurs as a result of changes in gene expression during the autumn, which is when the hares’ summer coats are replaced by their winter ones. The timing of this transition allows the hares to remain camouflaged from predators during the snowy winter.

[[☃ image 1]]

A group of scientists studied a certain snowshoe hare population in Western Montana for a period of three years. They found that, on average, the hares’ coats transitioned to white before the ground was fully covered in snow (Figure 2), which put the hares at risk for increased predation. This mismatch between molting time and snow cover was due to winter weather arriving later in the year than usual.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Based on the information above, which of the following statements is most likely true about the coat color transition in snowshoe hares?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 3]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Mills, L. Scott, Marketa Zimova, Jared Oyler, Steven Running, John T. Abatzoglou, and Paul M. Lukacs. 2013. "Camouflage mismatch in seasonal coat color due to decreased snow duration." *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences USA* 110(18): 7360-5. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222724110

*Left image credit: "Snowshoe Hare in Banff national park, near Peyto lake" by [Martin Kraft](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/User:Martin_Kraft) ([CC BY-SA 3.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/de/legalcode))*

*Right image credit: ["Snowshoe Hare (Lepus americanus), white morph, Shirleys Bay, Ottawa, Ontario, Canada"](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Snowshoe_Hare,_Shirleys_Bay.jpg) by [D. Gordon E. Robertson](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/User:Dger) ([CC BY-SA 3.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/deed.en))*

options.alt: Two photographs shown side by side. The photograph on the left is a brown colored rabbit in an area with green plants and dirt and the photograph on the right is a white rabbit in a snow covered area.
options.caption: **Figure 1.** A snowshoe hare with a brown coat in early autumn (left), and a snowshoe hare with a white coat in winter (right).

options.alt: The y axis of the line graph is labeled percent white coat and is marked at intervals of 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100. The x axis is labeled date and is marked at 9/17, 10/7, 10/27, 11/16, and 12/16. There is a line break on the x axis and then the markings begin again at 3/16 and continue at 4/5, 4/25, 5/15, 6/4 and 6/24. On the graph there is a vertical blue dotted line that intersects the x axis between 10/27 and 11/16, and the line is labeled start of snowy period. There is a second vertical blue dotted line that intersects the x axis between 4/25 and 5/15 and the line is labeled end of snowy period. The line begins at 9/17, 0 and doesn't begin to rise above 2 percent on the y axis until 10/7. At 10/7 the line begins to ascend sharply and reaches 80% at the dotted line that represents start of snowy period. There is a shaded gray region on the graph between the data line and the dotted line that represents the start of the snowy period. After the data line crosses the start of the snowy period, there is a minimal dip downward on the line, and then the line rises again at peaks at 11/16, 90. The line remains at 90% from 11/16 to approximately 4/15 on the x axis and then the line begins to sharply decline. The data line crosses the end of the snowy period line at 30 percent and then the line continues to descend and reaches 0 on the y axis at 6/4.
options.caption: **Figure 2.** Mismatch between coat color and snow cover. The black curve shows the average progression of coat color change for a hare in the population. The shaded area represents the time during which hare coat color and the color of the ground were significantly mismatched.

choices.content: The transition from a brown to a white coat is the result of gene expression changes induced by autumn daylengths.
The transition from a brown to a white coat is due to gene expression changes that are influenced by the amount of snow on the ground in late autumn.
The transition from brown to white coats occurs as the genes for having a white coat become more common in the population during the transition from fall to winter.
The transition from brown to white coats is an adaptation to climate change, which is predicted to shorten snow duration in the snowshoe hare’s habitat.

assessment_items.question_content:
*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, or baker’s yeast, is a unicellular fungus that can exist in either a haploid or diploid form. Haploid yeast can be one of two mating types: **a** or ⍺. When an **a** cell and an ⍺ cell are near each other they can mate and fuse, resulting in a diploid cell.

This mating process is initiated when a cell of one mating type senses pheromones produced by the opposite mating type. When this occurs, large-scale changes in gene expression lead to polarized growth, where one part of the cell grows outward. This cellular outgrowth is called a shmoo (Figure 1). Eventually, polarized growth in both mating types causes the haploid cells to make contact and fuse.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on the information above, which of the following statements is true?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: *S. cerevisiae* with a cellular outgrowth called a shmoo.
options.caption: **Figure 1.** *S. cerevisiae* with a shmoo.

choices.content: The switch to polarized growth is due to changes in gene expression induced by a signal from a yeast cell’s environment.
Shmoo formation occurs when a signal from the environment induces changes in the sequence of genes present in a haploid yeast cell.
Polarized growth is the result of gene expression changes that occur when **a** cells release ⍺ pheromones into the environment.
Changes in gene expression will lead to shmoo formation when an ⍺ cell detects the pheromones of another ⍺ cell.

assessment_items.question_content:
In the plant *Arabidopsis thaliana*, proteins called photoreceptors help sense the ratio of red to far-red wavelengths of light in the environment. When the environment is shady, the red:far-red ratio is low. 

If a plant’s photoreceptors detect that the plant is growing in shade, they initiate a change in phenotype called the shade avoidance response. During this response, changes in gene expression result in the plant growing taller and flowering earlier (Figure 1).

[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on the information above, which of the following statements is true?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A diagram of 2 plants in dirt filled containers. The container of the left is labeled bright light and there are green leaves in the container. The container on the right is labeled shade and there are green leaves in the container with a stem coming out of the container and flowers at the top of the stem.
options.caption: **Figure 1.** An illustration of *A. thaliana* phenotypes after growth for the same amount of time in bright light and shade.

choices.content: The switch to a shade avoidance response is the result of gene expression changes induced by low red:far-red light ratios.
The shade avoidance response results from light-induced changes in the sequence of genes present in an *A. thaliana* plant.
The shade avoidance response is the result of gene expression patterns associated with growing in an environment with a high red:far-red light ratio.
Changes in gene expression will lead to longer stems and earlier flowering when plants are moved from shade to bright light.

assessment_items.question_content:
In Australian central bearded dragons (*Pogona vitticeps*), sex is determined by a combination of genetics and environmental temperature. Genetically male lizards have two Z chromosomes (ZZ) and genetically female lizards have one Z chromosome and one W chromosome (ZW). When eggs are incubated at temperatures below $32\degree\text{C}$, ZZ embryos develop into phenotypic males, and ZW embryos develop into phenotypic females. However, when *P. vitticeps* eggs are incubated at temperatures greater than or equal to $32\degree\text{C}$, ZZ embryos can develop into phenotypic *females*, as shown in the following graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

A group of scientists is interested in the gene expression differences between phenotypic males and phenotypic females. They decide to investigate which of these differences can be attributed to temperature instead of genotype. So, the scientists design an experiment to compare the gene expression patterns in two groups of lizards.

**Based on the information above, which of the following comparisons should the scientists use for the experiment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Quinn, Alexander E., Arthur Georges, Stephen D. Sarre, Fiorenzo Guarino, Tariq Ezas, and Jennifer A. Marshall Graves. 2007. “Temperature Sex Reversal Implies Sex Gene Dosage in a Reptile.” *Science* 316(5823): 411. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1135925

options.alt: A bar graph of percent phenotypically female on the y axis vs. incubation temperature on the x axis. There is a key showing uppercase Z uppercase W as black bars, and uppercase Z uppercase Z as gray bars. uppercase Z uppercase W hatchlings are 100 percent phenotypically female at the four temperatures shown; 30 degrees Celsius, 32 degrees Celsius, 34 degrees Celsius, and 36 degrees Celsius. The percent phenotypically female for uppercase Z uppercase Z hatchlings is as follows: 30 degrees Celsius, 0 percent; 32 degrees Celsius, 10 percent; 34 degrees Celsius, 50 percent; and 36 degrees Celsius, 100 percent.
options.caption: **Figure 1.** Percent of hatchlings that are phenotypically female when incubated at different temperatures. Black and gray bars represent hatchlings with the ZW and ZZ genotypes, respectively.

choices.content: genetically male embryos incubated at $30\degree\text{C}$ compared to genetically male embryos incubated at $36\degree\text{C}$
genetically female embryos incubated at $30\degree\text{C}$ compared with genetically female embryos incubated at $36\degree\text{C}$
genetically male embryos incubated at $30\degree\text{C}$ compared with genetically male embryos incubated at $26\degree\text{C}$
genetically female embryos incubated at $30\degree\text{C}$ compared with genetically male embryos incubated at $26\degree\text{C}$

assessment_items.question_content:
*Daphnia cucullata* are tiny aquatic animals called water fleas. These animals have a protective covering on their head called a helmet, which can come in different sizes. 

A researcher predicts that helmet size in water fleas depends on chemical cues from predators in the environment rather than on inherited differences. To test this prediction, the researcher sets up two tanks which are represented in the diagram below. The researcher compares the helmet sizes of the water fleas raised in the control tank to those raised in the experimental tank.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following statements describes an essential part of the researcher’s experimental design?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Two images of rectangles filled with water and a dotted line dividing each rectangle in half. The image on the left is labeled control tank and the image on the right is labeled experimental tank. The image of the control tank shows 6 purple circles on the right side of the dotted line. The image of the experimental tank shows 6 blue triangles  on the left side of the dotted line, and 6 purple circles on the right side of the dotted line. Underneath the images there is a key indicating that the dotted line represents a nylon mesh barrier, the purple circles represent water fleas, and the blue triangles represent predator species.

choices.content: The water fleas used in the experiment must all have the same genotype.
The chemical that initiates helmet formation in water fleas must be isolated before the experiment is carried out.
The tanks must be filled with water taken from a lake where water fleas are naturally found.
The water fleas used must be at different stages of development at the start of the experiment.

assessment_items.question_content:
*Nemoria arizonaria* is a species of moth that lives in the southwestern United States. *N. arizonaria* caterpillars have a different phenotype depending on the season in which they are born. Caterpillars born in the spring develop yellow coloration and look like oak catkins, which are oak flowers. Caterpillars born in the summer develop grey-green coloration and look like oak twigs. 

A researcher claims that the phenotypes of *N. arizonaria* caterpillars depend primarily on diet rather than on inherited differences.

**Which of the following experiments will provide the most direct evidence to support the researcher’s claim?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Raising caterpillars of the same genotype under controlled conditions that differ only in the caterpillars’ food source
Crossing two moths that had different caterpillar phenotypes and calculating the ratio of catkin to twig phenotypes in the offspring
Determining the sequences of genes involved in *N. arizonaria* caterpillar morphology and comparing them to another species of caterpillar
Exposing genetically similar caterpillars to different temperatures and photoperiods during maturation

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements describes an example of phenotypic plasticity?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Two stems cut from a single *Hydrangea macrophylla* plant grow to have pink or purple flowers depending on soil pH.
The clownfish species *Amphiprion ocellaris* and *Amphiprion percula* have similar color patterns despite genetic differences.
Genetically identical *Arabidopsis thaliana* plants develop green stems when grown in low-light or in high-light conditions.
Two *Drosophila melanogaster* flies have different versions of a gene, causing one to have white eyes and the other to have red eyes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements describes an example of phenotypic plasticity?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Nodilittorina australis* snails with the same genotype can have either white or brown shells depending on the steepness of their rocky shore habitat.
The snake species *Pliocercus elapoides* and *Micrurus nigrocinctus* have similar color patterns despite genetic differences.
Genetically identical colonies of *Xanthomonas axonopodis* bacteria produce extracellular polysaccharides when grown on petri dishes at $25\degree\text{C}$ or at $28\degree\text{C}$.
Two maize plants have different versions of a gene, causing one to have flat leaves and the other to have bumpy leaves.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements describes an example of phenotypic plasticity?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Desert locusts with the same genotype develop into solitary or swarming forms depending on the density of individuals in their surroundings.
The common hawk-cuckoo and the shikra are types of birds with similar feather patterns and behaviors despite genetic differences.
Two stems cut from a single rose plant develop red flowers, despite one being planted in full sun and the other in shade.
A yeast cell with a genetic mutation grows into a pink colony on a petri dish, while a cell without the mutation grows into a white colony.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements describes an example of phenotypic plasticity?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A semi-aquatic plant produces large, thin submerged leaves and small, thick aerial leaves.
A sheep has long wool before being sheared and short wool afterwards.
A person develops a gene mutation in a healthy lung cell, resulting in the growth of a tumor.
A lizard has green skin when raised at $20\degree\text{C}$, and also at $25\degree\text{C}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements describes an example of phenotypic plasticity?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A person’s skin darkens in the sun due to the effect of UV light on melanin production.
A dog has a long coat before being groomed, and a short coat afterwards.
A certain substance in a person's environment causes a gene mutation, which leads to a normal cell becoming cancerous.
A potted plant produces pink flowers whether it’s watered once a day or twice a day.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements describes an example of phenotypic plasticity?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An arctic fox is brown in summer and white in winter.
A person has brown hair before applying hair dye, and purple hair afterwards.
A dog cell develops a mutation that changes the sequence of a gene, resulting in the growth of a tumor.
A cactus grows long spines in both drought and non-drought conditions.
